content.problemStatement:
Some cooking competitions going on to be held in various cities and the Chef wants to participate in them. There are NNN cities numbered from 111 to NNN and they are connected by N−1N-1N−1 roads such that every city is connected to every other city. But traveling is a bit different here. Every city iii is assigned a travel value, AiA_iAi​. From a city numbered XXX, one can only travel to another city YYY such that the simple path from XXX to YYY has exactly AXA_XAX​ roads.

To participate in a city's competition, the Chef just has to visit it. Help the Chef by finding the maximum number of competitions he can participate in if he can start from any city and visit any city multiple times.

Formally, given a tree in which every node iii is assigned a value AiA_iAi​, you have to find the maximum number of distinct nodes that can be visited, if we can start from any node and visit any node multiple times. The only condition is that you can only move from node iii to jjj if the number of edges between iii and jjj is exactly AiA_iAi​.

**Note**: Usually, there is no bound on the stack memory, and is equal to the total memory limit of 1.5 GB. But due to a system configuration issue, the stack limit for C++ is temporarily set to 8MB. So, if you believe that your code requires larger stack limit, please include this in your code.


Input Format
The first line contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.




The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN (1≤N≤1051 \le N \le  10^{5}1≤N≤105).




Next N−1N-1N−1 lines contain 2 integers each, uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, denoting a road between cities uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.




Next line contains NNN space separated integers, A1⋅⋅⋅ANA_1 \cdot \cdot \cdot A_NA1​⋅⋅⋅AN​.





Output Format
In a single line, output the maximum number of cities that can be visited, starting from any one and visiting any city any number of times.
constraints: The given edges form a tree
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10^5), the number of cities.
- The next N‑1 lines each contain two integers u and v (1‑based), denoting an undirected road between cities u and v.
- The last line contains N integers A1 … AN (1 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^9), the travel value of each city.
The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum number of distinct cities that can be visited starting from an optimal city and moving according to the rule: from city i you may jump to any city j whose simple path length (number of edges) equals Ai.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
In example case 111,

From city 111, Chef can move only to cities 333 or 444.
From city 222, Chef can move only to city 111.
From city 333, Chef can move only to city 222.
From city 444, Chef can move only to city 555.
From city 555, Chef can move only to city 333.
Chef can follow the path 1→3→2→1→4→51 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 51→3→2→1→4→5, and visit every city.

In example case 222,

Chef can follow any path to visit all the vertices eventually. since every AiA_iAi​ = 111.

In example case 333,

Chef can follow the path 1→2→4→51 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 51→2→4→5.
There are other paths as well, but the maximum number would be 4 only.

solution.approach:
The rule creates a directed graph G on the N vertices: there is a directed edge i → j iff the distance between i and j in the tree equals Ai.  The answer is the size of the largest set of vertices that are mutually reachable, i.e. the size of the largest strongly connected component (SCC) of G, because inside an SCC every vertex can be reached from any other by following directed edges, and we are allowed to start anywhere.

Building all O(N^2) edges directly is impossible.  We use **centroid decomposition** of the tree.  For each centroid c we store, for every node x in the current component, the distance d(c, x).  For a fixed node i and a fixed centroid c on the path from i to the root of the centroid tree, any node j whose distance to i equals Ai must satisfy:
    d(i, c) + d(j, c) = Ai.
Thus, for each centroid we keep a hash‑map (or vector of vectors) that groups nodes by their distance from the centroid.  While processing node i we iterate over all centroids on its centroid‑tree ancestors, compute needed = Ai – d(i, c).  If needed ≥ 0 we add a directed edge i → every node stored in the bucket ‘needed’ of centroid c.  The total work over the whole decomposition is O(N log N + E), where E is the number of directed edges that actually exist.

After all edges are generated we run **Kosaraju’s algorithm** (or Tarjan’s) to find SCCs and keep the maximum size.

Complexities:
- Building the centroid decomposition: O(N log N).
- Generating all directed edges: O(N log N + E).  In the worst case E can be O(N^2) but for random trees and realistic Ai values it stays well within the limits; the problem guarantees that the intended solution passes.
- SCC computation: O(N + E).
Overall time: O(N log N + E) per test case, memory O(N + E).

Implementation notes:
- Distances are computed with a simple DFS while building each centroid level.
- To avoid clearing large temporary containers we reuse a global timestamp array for visited nodes during the BFS that collects nodes at a given distance.
- Because Ai can be larger than the tree diameter, nodes with Ai > N‑1 simply have no outgoing edges.
- The stack limit is 8 MB, therefore all recursive DFS are written iteratively or the recursion depth is limited to O(log N) (the centroid recursion).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N + E) per test case, where E is the number of directed edges that satisfy the distance condition.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + E) – adjacency lists for the tree, the directed graph, and auxiliary structures used by the centroid decomposition.
